Since I have no investment in this pot beyond the initial blind and one small flop call, checking is the most prudent play to see the showdown without risking further chips on a low-probability bluff.

I'm holding pocket sevens (7h7d) in UTG position with a healthy stack (100 BB). Pocket pairs are solid starting hands that should be played aggressively from early position. My recent table activity suggests I've been reasonably active but disciplined. With four players and no action before me, raising represents the standard play here. I'll raise to 300 (3x BB) to build the pot, define my hand strength, and potentially take down the blinds. If called, I'll have decent position and can evaluate the flop. The hand is too strong to fold and merely calling would be passive and invite too many opponents. This raise also keeps me aggressive and consistent with my table image from recent hands.

Claude opened UTG to 300 in a short‑handed pot — that’s a fairly strong, ahead-of-range open. 8♥3♥ is a very weak suited connector and is often dominated; calling 300 into a 450 pot requires ~40% equity, which this hand doesn’t have vs an UTG opening range. A 3‑bet bluff here is marginal at best (UTG will call or 4‑bet too often), and playing multiway out of position with 8‑3s is unprofitable. I’ll conserve chips for stronger spots and folds.

I am in the Small Blind holding King of Clubs and Ten of Spades. Claude has raised to 300 from UTG in this 4-handed game. KTo is a solid starting hand in a short-handed format, although it can be difficult to play out of position. Given the pot odds (250 to call into a pot of 450) and Claude's active history in previous hands, I have enough equity to see a flop. While a 3-bet is an option, calling keeps the pot manageable while I am out of position against an aggressive opponent.

Preflop in the big blind with QTo offsuit facing an UTG raise from Claude to 300 and a flat call from Flash in the small blind. The pot is 700, and it costs 200 to call, offering about 3.5:1 odds. However, QTo is a marginal hand with poor postflop playability out of position, especially against a likely strong UTG range from Claude and Flash's calling range. Defending wide OOP risks difficult spots ahead. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in this deep-stack cash game.

I have King-high with some backdoor potential (one spade for a flush draw and overcards) on a low, somewhat connected board. As the first to act against the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the standard play. I want to see how Claude reacts to this board before committing more chips with just overcards.

I'm holding pocket sevens (7h7d) and the flop came 7s 9c 4s - I've flopped a strong set of sevens. Flash, who called my preflop raise, has checked the flop. The board is relatively dry with no obvious draws or connected cards that would have helped most ranges. 

This is an excellent flop for my hand, and I should bet for value to build the pot and extract chips. Flash's check suggests weakness - they likely missed or have a marginal hand. A bet around 450 (approximately 65% of the 700 pot) is well-sized to: 1) extract value from weaker hands that might call, 2) build the pot for future streets, and 3) price out drawing hands while still allowing Flash to continue with worse holdings like mid-pair or ace-high hands. With my set, I'm well-positioned to win this hand and should be aggressive in building the pot.

On a flop of 7s-9c-4s, my King-high with one backdoor spade (Ts) has very little equity against a standard UTG opening range. Claude has been active and is following up his pre-flop lead with a continuation bet of 450 into a 700 pot. To call, I would need roughly 28% equity. Given I am out of position and have completely missed this board with no significant draws, it is better to fold and conserve my stack rather than floating a bet with only overcards.

4-handed table — open-raise much wider than full-ring. A♣4♣ is a playable suited Ace with good postflop playability and decent blockers; I want to take the initiative, isolate the blinds and get fold equity. Standard open to ~3x the big blind keeps the pot manageable against deep stacks but applies pressure to SB/BB who have shown aggression in previous hands. Plan: open to 300; if I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold to preserve stack, but I’ll consider calling smaller 3-bets from the button depending on who it is and sizing. Raising also reduces multiway disadvantage vs limping.

I am on the button with Q7 offsuit. Minni, who is UTG, has opened for 300. Q7o is a weak hand to play against an early position raise, even with the benefit of being on the button. I'll fold and wait for a better spot to avoid losing chips unnecessarily.

K2 suited is a marginal hand at best, with poor playability out of position in the small blind. Facing an UTG raise from Minni, who has shown aggression but a strong opening range, my equity is low against their likely holdings like high pairs, broadway cards, or suited connectors. Pot odds to call 250 into 450 are about 2:1 (needing ~33% equity), but K2s realizes far less OOP multiway potentially. No fold equity with a squeeze given my weak blocker and position. Best to fold and preserve stack for better spots, as the long-term EV is negative here.

I'm in the big blind facing a 300 raise from Minni in UTG position. I hold Td Qd, which is a decent broadway hand with good playability. The pot odds are favorable (450:200 = 2.25:1, needing ~31% equity), and TQo has approximately 40-45% equity against a typical UTG range. While I'm out of position post-flop, the hand strength and pot odds justify a call here. Folding would be too passive, and raising risks committing more chips with a marginal hand. Calling allows me to see the flop and leverage my hand strength against Minni's range while maintaining flexibility.

I'm holding Td Qd on a 3s Kc 2s flop against Minni's UTG raise. While I have broadway cards with decent equity (~40-45%) and outs to improve, I currently have no made hand. The King on the board likely hits Minni's strong UTG range heavily. Checking is the optimal play because: (1) I want to control the pot size with a medium-strength hand, (2) I'm last to act post-flop which gives me positional advantage, (3) Betting Queen-high is marginal without showdown value, and (4) This line allows me to see more cards and gather information about Minni's holdings before committing more chips. The pot odds are reasonable to continue, but I should do so passively to manage my risk.

I opened to 300 preflop and Claude just called. Flop is 3♠ K♣ 2♠ and I have A♣4♣ — just Ace-high but with backdoor club potential and the Ace blocker. This is a fairly dry board and, as the preflop aggressor with position, a small continuation bet is the standard play to pick up the pot and get value from worse hands/draws. I’ll bet ~1/3 pot (220 into the 650) to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable. Plan: take it down if he folds; if he calls, re-evaluate the turn with my backdoor equity; fold to a large check-raise since I currently have no pair.

Pot odds of 3.95:1 require only ~20% equity to break even. With Td Qd against Minni's UTG range on a 3s Kc 2s flop, I have approximately 28-32% equity including one overcard (Q), pair-making outs, and backdoor draws. The bet size is reasonable, and calling preserves position advantage for future streets while maintaining profitable expected value.

The turn brings a 4s to a board of 3s Kc 2s 4s. I hold Td Qd - currently just queen-high with no made hand. The 4s doesn't improve my holdings (no straight draw, no significant equity improvement). Against Minni's UTG raising range, I have approximately 23-25% equity with limited outs. Minni checked to me on the turn, and given my weak hand, checking is the optimal play because: (1) I avoid bloating the pot with queen-high against a likely strong range, (2) I preserve the ability to see the river cheaply, (3) queen-high has some showdown value but insufficient equity to warrant a value bet, and (4) checking allows me to gather more information on the river while maintaining control of the pot size. This passive approach is consistent with my medium-strength holding and out-of-position situation.

I improved to a weak pair on the turn (A4 — paired my 4) after opening preflop and firing a small c‑bet on the flop. Claude called the flop bet, so his range likely contains Kx, small pocket pairs (2s, 3s, 5–9 pairs), and some floats. The turn brought a third spade, so some hands (two spades in hole) already have a flush, and single‑spade combos can still make a river flush — but many of Claude’s calling combos are still worse than my pair. As the preflop aggressor I should continue to apply pressure: a bet extracts value from worse pairs and middling Kx hands, charges single‑spade combos and any turn‑pair floaters, and preserves fold equity. I’ll size to just over half the pot (600) — big enough to charge draws and get value from worse, but small enough that a large check‑raise can be folded without committing a big portion of my stack. If he folds I take it; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river; if he check‑raises heavily I’ll likely concede this marginal pair.

Folding queen-high on the turn. Minni has shown consistent aggression (raise pre-flop, raise flop, raise turn) suggesting a strong range. My Td Qd has no made hand, no meaningful draws (board has 3 spades already), and approximately 15-20% equity against her range. Pot odds of 2.82:1 require 26% equity to break even, which I don't have. This is an unprofitable call that preserves my stack for better spots.

I am in the UTG position with Ten-Deuce suited. This is a very weak hand to open from early position, even in a four-handed game. It is easily dominated and does not play well in multi-way pots or against aggression from the button or blinds. My best course of action is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Preflop on the button with pocket threes after UTG fold. Excellent position facing only blinds. Small pocket pairs like 33 are strong speculative hands here due to set-mining potential (about 11.8% chance to flop a set) and play well postflop in position. Standard play is to raise for value/thin value/isolation against blinds, building a pot while taking initiative. Common sizing is 3x BB (300). Deep stacks allow implied odds if we hit. Folding or limping is suboptimal; raising balances bluffs and value. Opponents from history: Claude and Minni defend wide but we've won pots raising from button before.

Pocket sixes is a solid hand to play from the SB against Elon's button raise. At 9830 chips, I have good stack depth to realize implied odds if I hit a set. The pot is 450 and I only need to call 250 more, giving me favorable immediate odds. Elon has been aggressive from the button throughout the session, so his range is likely quite wide (broadway cards, pocket pairs, broadway-low kicker combos). Pocket sixes is ahead of many of his likely holdings. A 3-bet risks inflating the pot with a small pair out of position, especially with Minni (chip leader with 14070) still to act in the BB. Calling allows me to see the flop in position relative to Elon and make an informed decision post-flop. If the flop brings a six, I have a strong hand with plenty of value to extract.

Facing Elon’s raise to 300 and Claude’s call, I’m in the big blind with J♣6♥ — a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability and a high chance of being dominated. Pot is 700 and it costs me 200 to continue (200/(700+200) ≈ 22% pot odds). Multiway action (open plus caller) significantly reduces my equity and puts me out-of-position postflop against at least one active opponent. A small raise to 500 would commit chips into a pot I’m unlikely to win and a larger 3-bet is unjustified with this holding. With healthy stack depth I can wait for a much better spot. Fold and preserve stack for profitable situations.

Checking with pocket sixes on Qd 5c 3s flop. My hand is vulnerable to Elon's wide button raise range, which likely contains Queens, overcards, and better pairs. The Queen is particularly concerning. By checking, I keep the pot manageable, gain information about his hand strength, and avoid overcommitting with a marginal holding. If Elon checks back, I see the turn cheaply. If he bets, I can make an informed fold or call with position to come on later streets.

Flopped top set with 3d3c on a Qd5c3s rainbow board, which is extremely strong—likely the nuts, only beaten by improbable hands like 44+ or quads. Board is dry with no flush draws and minimal straight draw potential (A42 unlikely). Claude's preflop call from SB is wide, and check indicates weakness or trapping, but betting extracts value from Qx, overpairs (AA/KK/JJ), mid pairs, or any Ax. Deep stacks provide huge implied odds already realized. Half-pot bet builds pot without scaring off one-pair hands while maintaining aggression from preflop raiser image. History shows opponents call bets with marginal holdings.

Calling 400 with pocket sixes on Qd 5c 3s. Getting 3.75:1 pot odds with ~30% equity against Elon's wide aggressive range. Elon's limited stack (6250) means set value is substantial - if I hit, he's likely pot-committed. The dry flop texture keeps my outs clean. I can fold on the turn if I miss and Elon continues, making this a reasonable expectation play that balances set value with manageable risk.

Elon checked on the turn, and I need to decide whether to bet or check with pocket sixes on a board of Qd 5c 3s Kc.

Analysis:
- My hand is an underpair to both the Queen and King on board
- Elon raised the flop but checked the turn, suggesting either weakness or a hand he wants to control
- My equity is likely only 20-30% - I'm vulnerable to most of Elon's range that includes QQ, KK, AQ, AK, QJ, etc.
- Betting here makes no sense with such marginal equity. I'd be bluffing without a strong plan, and Elon showed flop aggression, so he's unlikely to fold to a turn bet from an aggressive opponent
- The turn check is actually helpful - it gives me a chance to see the river at no cost
- If Elon completely missed (unlikely given his flop bet), checking allows him to bluff the river where I can call
- If he has a made hand, I can fold to a river bet and save chips

Checking preserves my stack and gives me a cheap look at the river. With such a vulnerable holding and unfavorable equity, minimizing losses is the priority.

Top set on the flop remains extremely strong on this turn card (Kc), which doesn't complete any obvious draws—board is Qd5c3sKc, rainbow-ish with no flush, straight draws are gutshots at best (e.g., JT, 46). Claude's line (preflop defend wide from SB, check-call flop, check turn) screams medium strength like Qx, Kx draw that hit, overpairs (AA/JJ/TT?), or two-pair (KQ perfect). No reason to slowplay with nuts; betting extracts value from these while protecting against gutshots or runner-runner threats. Deep stacks (my 6250, his 9180) support betting. 2/3 pot (1000 into 1500) balances value/targeting calls without overbetting vs. a calling station per history. Folding or checking risks giving free cards or missing value.

Folding pocket sixes on Qd 5c 3s Kc turn facing a 1000 raise. My hand is significantly vulnerable as an underpair to both the Queen and King on board. Elon's line (flop bet to 400, turn raise to 1000) indicates real strength from his position - likely Broadway cards (AK, AQ, QJ), made pairs (QQ, KK), or a strong draw. Even getting 2.5:1 pot odds (needing 28.5% equity), my actual equity against his range is only 15-25% at best. Pocket sixes rarely win in multiway pots on coordinated boards, and the river is the final street with no information-gathering opportunities. This is a clear fold - preserving stack preservation for premium spots is critical in tournament-style play. Elon's 5250 remaining chips suggest he's likely committed to the hand, and I don't have the equity to justify the call. Minimize losses with weak holdings on dangerous boards.

Preflop UTG in a 4-handed game with 5s9h offsuit, a weak hand with poor playability, no straight or flush potential, dominated by better hands in any calling range. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots. Pot is small (150), to call 100 into multiway potential OOP is -EV. Opponent tendencies show aggression postflop, but this hand doesn't play well against them.

Strong premium hand (TsKs) in the best position (Button). King-Ten suited is in the top tier of starting hands. Elon has folded, leaving Minni and Flash. A standard raise to 300 (3x BB) from the button is appropriate here to take control of the pot, build value with my strong hand, and narrow the field. This is a straightforward aggressive play in position with a premium holding.

I’m in the small blind with Th 2s facing a button open to 300 (pot 450). To call I must put in 250, which means I need ~250/(450+250)=~36% equity to break even. Ten-deuce offsuit is poorly positioned and is often dominated by a button opener’s range; it has very weak post‑flop playability out of position and is a poor candidate for a light 3‑bet given a competent button and a live big blind behind. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left to play, folding and preserving stack is the +EV play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h3h. Claude, on the button, has raised to 300. 93-offsuit is a very weak hand, even when closing the action against a single raiser from the button. Out of position, this hand will be difficult to play and is well behind Claude's button opening range. Given the weak nature of the hand, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

8-4 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker with very limited equity and poor playability. From UTG (earliest position) with three opponents yet to act behind me, this is a clear fold. Position is critical in poker, and I should only play premium hands from early position. The 100 chip call is not worth the weak holding, as I'll be out of position postflop against the remaining players. This aligns with my recent tight play from early positions which has been profitable.

I have 2♣7♣ on the button facing the posted small and big blinds. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop (min raise to 200). 2-7 suited is the single worst starting hand — it fares poorly against blind-defending ranges and is frequently dominated. Calling requires ~40% equity to be break-even right away; vs two blind defenders my realistic equity is well below that and raising to attempt a steal puts me in marginal multi-way/3-bet spots against aggressive opponents (Elon in particular has shown frequent preflop aggression). With a healthy 14k stack and many hands left, this is not a spot to force action with the worst hand — fold and wait for a better situation.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-5 offsuit. The action has folded to me. While I could potentially complete the small blind for 50 more, 8-5 offsuit is a very weak hand and generally not worth playing even from the small blind against Elon in the Big Blind, who has shown a willingness to be aggressive in previous hands. Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve my stack.

6♥2♦ is one of the weakest UTG hands. I’m first to act into three players (button, SB, BB) — very likely to be dominated and I’ll be out of position postflop. Calling 100 to limp into a multiway pot with almost no equity and poor playability is a negative EV line; raising is pointless here. With a healthy stack I’ll preserve chips for better spots. Fold.

I am on the button with 8h 9s. This is a marginal hand that doesn't play particularly well against potential calls from the blinds, especially since Claude has shown a willingness to defend and Elon has been quite active. In a four-handed game, 89 offsuit is near the bottom of a button opening range. Folding here is a disciplined play to wait for a better spot.

Preflop, unopened pot after UTG and Button folds. In SB with K9s, a strong suited king hand with good playability and blocker to KK. BB defends wide, so raise to steal blinds or build pot heads-up. Standard sizing 3x BB to 300 total. Deep stacks allow for postflop flexibility. Folding or limping is suboptimal.

Folding 2h-8c from the big blind. This is one of the worst starting hands in poker - weak, offsuit, and has minimal equity against Elon's raising range. While I'm getting 2:1 odds (200 to call into 400), the hand is fundamentally too weak to continue. My recent results show I've been bleeding chips by calling with marginal hands and losing contested pots. Preservation of chips for premium hands is critical when 110 hands remain. Elon has shown aggression throughout the session, and my hand doesn't have the strength to profitably engage. Fold is the disciplined, mathematically correct play.

I am in the UTG position with Ace-Trey offsuit. In a 4-handed game, the hand value increases slightly, but Ace-Trey offsuit is still a marginal hand from early position. However, since the table has been somewhat active with raises and I have a decent stack (88.5 BB), I'll put in a standard open raise to 300 to take control of the pot and represent strength. If I get re-raised, I can comfortably fold given the weak kicker.

JQ offsuit on the button facing a single UTG raise to 300 into a 450 pot. This is a standard defending spot in position with a hand that has good playability, decent equity, and blockers to strong hands like KQ, AJ. Position is huge advantage postflop. Calling gives good pot odds (300 to win 750), and we can realize equity well. Folding is too tight; 3-betting is possible but risks playing OOP if reraised, and JQo calls well preflop. Opponents are deep-stacked, but history shows Flash opens variably, so calling exploits position.

With 8d2s in the small blind facing 250 to call, I should fold this trash hand. This is one of poker's worst holdings - unpaired, unconnected, weak kickers with no real potential. Flash raised from UTG (early position strength indicator) and Elon called from the button. Even with 3:1 pot odds (750 to 250), I cannot justify calling with 8-2 offsuit. The hand will flop a pair only ~20% of the time, and even when it does, my hand will be vulnerable against likely stronger holdings. I'm out of position postflop and facing two opponents who have already shown interest. Folding preserves chips and avoids a difficult, likely losing situation.

Fold. Facing an UTG raise to 300 with the button calling, this is a multiway pot and I’m in the big blind with Q♠2♦ — a weak, unsuited, unconnected hand that plays poorly out of position. Calling 200 into a 750 pot requires ~21% equity (200/(750+200) ≈ 21%), but against an UTG open and a calling button I’m often dominated by Qx, Ax, and pocket pairs and have poor postflop playability. A small squeeze to 500 would be a thin/isolation attempt with a junk hand and likely to get called or put in tough spots. With a healthy stack I’ll fold and wait for a better, cleaner spot.

I have bottom pair (pair of 3s) with an Ace kicker on a relatively dry board of 9h-2h-3d. I am out of position against Elon, who called my pre-flop raise. A continuation bet is standard here to maintain the initiative and see where I stand. While bottom pair isn't exceptionally strong, it has some value and I want to put pressure on his likely overcards or heart draws. I'll bet roughly half the pot to keep the price for a draw expensive and to potentially end the hand right here.

Flop is 9h 2h 3d, a relatively dry board with two hearts for flush draw potential and low coordinated cards. JQo offsuit has queen-high overcards, no pair, no straight or flush draw, but decent blockers to strong broadway hands like KQ/AQ. Preflop, we defended wide in position vs UTG open, which is standard. Flash's UTG range is strong but c-bets frequently from history (aggressive player who bets flops often). Facing a 400 bet into 1150 pot (~28% pot), pot odds are ~2.9:1 direct, plus significant implied odds, position for postflop control, and ability to fold to turn aggression or bluff-catch/showdown. Folding is too tight given position and price; raising as a bluff risks variance vs his likely calling range (sets, two-pair, overpairs, draws). Calling realizes equity from overcards (~6 outs to two overs), backdoor potential, and exploits position to steal turns or value bet if improves. Plan: call, evaluate turn (check most turns unless improves, fold to heavy aggression unless board helps).

On the turn, a King of Clubs appears. I currently hold bottom pair (pair of 3s) with an Ace kicker. This King is a scare card that hits the range of a flat-caller like Elon, who could easily have hands like K-Q, K-J, or K-10. Since my continuation bet on the flop was called, and now an overcard has arrived, my relative hand strength has decreased. I will check to control the size of the pot and see how Elon reacts. If he bets, I will likely fold as my bottom pair is very vulnerable and I have little equity to improve.

Turn brings Kc, pairing the board somewhat indirectly but bringing a king which hits Flash's UTG range hard (KQ, AK, etc.) and improves many overpairs or broadways. My JQo is still queen-high with no pair, no straight (JQ doesn't connect well: possible runner-runner but nothing now), no flush draw. Preflop and flop call was for position, playability, and overcard equity, which didn't realize (queen outs somewhat live but Kc reduces broadway strength). Flash's flop c-bet continuation was standard, now checking turn: could be weak (missed draw, float), pot control with medium strength (overpair, top pair), or trap with monster (set, two-pair). Betting queen-high risks getting called by better (Kx, sets) or raised; thin value unlikely vs UTG who bets strong hands. Bluffing possible but board uncoordinated enough that he can call/raise wide; JQ blocks some of his calls (KQ, AQ less likely). Best to check: realize cheap showdown value (beats pure bluffs/air), control pot, see river free (possibly peel draws or blank), maintain range balance. Postflop position key; no need to bluff here when checked to with marginal hand—save bluffs for river if he checks again or worse boards. Folding impossible. Plan aligns with pre-turn thoughts: check unimproved turns.

On the river, the board has paired Kings (Kc Kd), making the board 9h-2h-3d-Kc-Kd. My hole cards are Ac-3s, which gives me two pair: Kings and Treys with an Ace kicker. However, this is a relatively weak hand because any pocket pair (44-88) or any 9 in Elon's range now has a better two pair than mine (Kings and 9s, or Kings and their pocket pair). Elon called my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet, which strongly suggests he has a piece of the board or a pocket pair. When the first King hit the turn and we both checked, it didn't change much. The second King on the river counterfeits my small pair if Elon has any pair higher than treys. Since I am out of position and my hand has limited showdown value against his likely range, I will check and hope to reach showdown cheaply or fold if he puts in a significant bet.

River pairs the kings with Kd on a board of 9h 2h 3d Kc Kd. My JQo remains queen-high with no improvement, no pair, no straight or flush draw completed. Flash's UTG range is strong: favors AK/AQ/KQ/KK/overpairs TT+, sets, strong draws preflop. Flop c-bet into dry board suggests continuation with that range. Checking turn Kc likely pot control with top pair/weak kicker, overpair, or missed draw; now river Kd pairs board kings, improving his Kx to kings + kicker (AK best, KQ good), potentially full houses or quads rare. Checking river suggests weakness (missed draws like hearts/straights unlikely: straight draws were 45/A4/T5 rare in UTG), pot control with marginal made hands (overpairs QQ-JJ now vulnerable), or trap with nuts (unlikely twice). Betting Q-high risks value-owning vs any Kx (huge portion of range), getting called/raised by better; bluff equity poor as board paired, he calls wide with top pair+, blocks few calls (KQ/AQ somewhat). Position allows cheap showdown, beating his bluffs/air (possible after double check-back), QQ/JJ/T9 if in range. Folding impossible. Checking exploits his check (avoids bluffcatching bet), realizes equity at no cost, balances range (had calls with draws/pairs earlier). Consistent with preflop/flop/turn strategy of positional play without overcommitting unimproved.

J6d is a marginal suited connector with some speculative value for draws, but from UTG in a 4-handed game, it's too weak to open or limp. Standard play is to fold early position trash to maintain a tight-aggressive range and wait for stronger hands like high pairs, broadway, or better suited connectors. Opponents have shown aggression, so folding avoids difficult post-flop spots OOP with a weak hand. Stack is deep at 7200, no need to gamble here early in the session (hand 43/150).

With A7o on the button, I'm in a favorable position. After Elon folded UTG, the action is back to me needing to call 100 into a 150 chip pot. 

Key considerations:
1. Position: On the button - I'll act last post-flop with information advantage
2. Card strength: Ace-Seven is decent, not premium but playable with the Ace giving strong equity
3. Pot odds: 150:100 (1.5:1), need roughly 40% equity - A7o has better than that against most ranges
4. Stack dynamics: I have 9180, Minni has 13870, Flash has 9600 - all reasonable for post-flop play
5. Opponents: Minni aggressive, Flash moderate - both likely have various holdings in SB/BB

This is a straightforward call. I'll see the flop in position against blinds, with decent hand equity and the ability to leverage my button position post-flop for decision-making advantage.

J♣7♥ is a weak, unsuited, out-of-position hand facing a button limp and the BB. Pot is 250, calling costs only 50 and min-raise is to 200, but both calling and a small raise leave me in tough multiway/OOP spots against ranges that dominate J7o. A min-raise likely gets called and won’t reliably isolate; calling 50 to play 3-way is a poor long-term spot. Preserve stack for better spots in the 150-hand session — fold and concede the small blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 4d. Elon folded UTG, Claude (the Button) limped in, and Minni (the Small Blind) folded. As I am in the Big Blind and there is no raise, I have the option to check and see a free flop. My hand is weak and does not warrant a raise against a limp from the Button, who could be trapping or just playing a wide range. I will take the free flop and see if I can connect with the board.

I have bottom pair (fours) on a relatively coordinated board with two Broadway cards (Queen and Ten) and a flush draw. I was the Big Blind and checked pre-flop after Claude limped on the button. Since I am first to act on the flop, checking is the most prudent play. My hand has some showdown value but is very vulnerable, and betting doesn't accomplish much against a range that likely includes many overcards or better pairs. I'll check and see how Claude reacts.

Flash checked to me on a 4s Qc Tc flop. I'm on the button with A7o after calling 100 pre-flop, so this is a standard continuation bet spot. 

Key factors:
1. Position advantage: I'm last to act, Flash has already shown weakness with a check
2. Hand strength: A7o gives me top pair with a strong ace kicker - this is above average on this board
3. Board texture: Q-T-4-4 is relatively coordinated but not particularly draw-heavy; Flash's check likely indicates a whiff, weak pair, or medium hand
4. Betting: A standard c-bet of ~150-160 chips (about 60% of pot) is appropriate here to build the pot with my strong hand and likely take it down immediately
5. Stack dynamics: All players have healthy stacks for continued play, so no need to be conservative

This is a textbook continuation bet - I have a strong hand, I'm in position, my opponent showed weakness, and the flop doesn't heavily favor their range.

I have bottom pair on a very coordinated board with two overcards (Queen and Ten) and a flush draw. Claude limped from the button and is now betting 160 into a 250 pot. While I have some equity, my hand is extremely vulnerable to almost any turn card, and I am out of position. Given the board texture and my weak kicker, it is unlikely that my bottom pair will remain the best hand by the river, and calling here often leads to more difficult decisions on later streets. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Q♥2♦ is one of the weakest hands in poker. From UTG (earliest position), I have no positional advantage and all opponents can act after me. The hand has poor equity, weak kicker potential, and no strong draw possibilities. With a healthy stack (9,330), I should fold and wait for better opportunities. This is standard early position play - only play premium hands from UTG.

I’m on the button with Q♦9♥. Pot is 150 and it’s 100 to call; min-raise to 200 is available. With position and deep stacks this is a good spot to open a wider stealing range — Q9o is marginal but profitable to raise here rather than limp. A standard 2xBB (200 total) sizing picks up the blinds often, keeps the pot manageable, and preserves postflop maneuverability. Plan: fold to a large, polarized 3-bet; consider calling a small 3-bet depending on sizing and opponent; if called, use position to c-bet/pressure favorable boards and probe for reads.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack of Spades and Seven of Clubs (J7o). Minni on the Button has opened with a min-raise to 200. J7o is a very weak hand to play out of position, even against a standard button opening range. Given the relatively shallow effective stacks and the fact that I'll be out of position for the remainder of the hand, the best play is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 200 (2x) after UTG fold and SB fold. Pot 350, call 100 (25% pot odds). Hand Jd3c is offsuit, disconnected, poor playability postflop. Vs wide button range (~35-40% hands), equity ~35-40%, but realization low and postflop difficult OOP with reverse position. Minni opens frequently from late position per history, but J3o below standard defending range (prefers suited connectors, broadway offsuit like JTo+, Ax). 3bet bluff possible but blockers weak, prefer better spots. Standard fold to avoid marginal OOP situations and preserve stack.

UTG with 8♦6♠ — a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. There’s 150 in the pot and 100 to call (just the big blind), which would be a limp into 3 players including aggressive opponents (Elon and Claude) who often raise/3‑bet. This hand is frequently dominated and has poor equity and implied odds out of position; calling requires ~40% equity vs the field to break even, which 8‑6o does not have. Raising to try and steal is too loose from UTG and will likely get punished. Fold and wait for a better spot to leverage my deep stack.

I am on the button with Ace-Four offsuit. The action has folded to me. While A4o is a relatively weak hand, it is strong enough to open for a raise from the button in a 4-handed game to try and steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I will raise to 3x the big blind.

Pocket nines is a premium hand preflop, especially against a button raise which is likely wide and stealing-oriented. From the small blind, flatting risks multiway pots and poor postflop position. 3-betting isolates the button, applies pressure on the big blind (Claude), and builds the pot with a strong hand that wants action. Standard sizing is 3-3.5x the raise (300), so raising to 900. Opponent stacks are deep enough, and history shows Flash and Claude call/raise but not overly tight.

Folding A2o to a 3-bet. This is one of the worst hands in poker and plays poorly in multiway pots. Even with decent pot odds (2.6:1), my hand has insufficient equity against Elon's 3-bet range to justify the call. Preserving my 9230 stack for premium hands is the correct strategic choice.

I opened from the button with Ace-Four offsuit, and Elon in the small blind has 3-bet to 900. While I have the benefit of position, A4o is a very weak hand to defend against a 3-bet. It is frequently dominated by better Aces (A5-AK) and has poor post-flop playability. Given the pot odds and the range Elon is likely representing from the small blind, a fold is the most disciplined and standard play to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position with Ten of Hearts and Four of Clubs. This is a very weak hand, and opening from this position with such a range would be unprofitable in the long run. There is no reason to enter the pot, so I will fold.

On the button with 7s4s after UTG fold in a 4-handed game. This is a standard open-raise spot. 74 suited is playable from the button due to position, suited connectivity for straight/flush potential, and high fold equity against the blinds. Raising to 3x the BB (300 total) is standard sizing for isolation/steal. Postflop playability in position is strong with deep stacks. Folding would miss value; limping is suboptimal.

Folding K7o from the small blind against Elon's raise. This is a marginal hand that's easily dominated in a multi-way pot or heads-up play. Minni still has to act behind me, and K7 lacks the strength to profitably defend or aggress. Early in the session, stack preservation and hand selection discipline are crucial. Folding to wait for stronger holdings.

I have 3♦6♠ in the big blind facing a button open to 300 (pot = 450, I must call 200 more). 3-6o is very weak and is heavily dominated by a typical BTN opening range; it has poor postflop playability out of position. Calling requires ~30.8% equity (200/(450+200)), which 3-6o rarely achieves vs a BTN open, and a 3-bet bluff here is thin (no useful blockers and would bloat a pot OOP). Preserve my deep stack for better spots — fold and save chips for more playable hands.

UTG with T7o is a clear fold in a 4-handed cash game. This hand has poor playability, no high card strength, offsuit, and low equity against any raising range. Position is early, stacks are deep (~75BB+), so no need to speculate. Opponent tendencies from history don't justify entering with this; better to wait for premium hands to build stack long-term.

K5o is too weak to play from the button. While I have position postflop and immediate pot odds of 1.5:1, the hand lacks playability against two opponents. K5 has poor equity preflop and would rarely connect profitably. Disciplined fold to preserve stack for stronger opportunities.

I’m in the small blind with 6c4h heads-up versus the big blind. The pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to complete; min-raise is 200. 6-4 offsuit is a weak, marginal hand and I’ll be out of position postflop regardless of opening. Folding my posted blind is too tight; raising to 200 with this junk hand would bloat the pot and invite 3-bets without much equity. Calling (completing to 100 total) gives me ~3:1 pot odds (only ~25% equity needed) to see a flop and keeps the pot manageable. Plan: see the flop and play for pot control OOP — continue with any pair, good draw, or improvement; fold to large aggression when missed.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit in the Big Blind. The Small Blind (Minni) has just completed the blind (limped). AQ is a premium hand, especially in a blind-vs-blind situation. Checking is an option to keep the pot small, but raising for value is the standard and more profitable play here. Raising allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win it immediately or build it for a post-flop advantage with a strong hand. I'll raise to 400 total (a 4x increase) to thin the field and extract value.

I’m in the small blind with 6♣4♥ facing a raise to 400 from the big blind. I’ve already contributed 100, so calling costs me +300 (total commitment 400). 6‑4o is a weak, out-of-position hand — folding the posted blind is too tight and 3‑betting would bloat the pot and invite stronger action with little equity. Calling keeps the pot manageable, gives ~3:1 pot odds to see a flop, and lets me play for pot control: continue with any pair, good draw, or clear improvement; fold to heavy aggression when missed. Given stack depths and opponent behavior, a simple call is the best line.

I have two pair (6s and 4s) on 4♣ T♥ 6♥ — a very strong made hand heads-up versus the preflop raiser. Flash’s raising range is wide, so many of his holdings (T-x, K-x, A-x, smaller pocket pairs, suited connectors) are behind me or are draws. I should extract value and charge heart/straight draws rather than slow-play. A ~3/4-pot bet (~600 into 800) will get called by worse one-pair hands and medium pairs, and will charge/remove many draws while still leaving room to fold to extreme action if he check‑raises big (which usually represents a set or the narrow two‑pair combos that beat me). If called, I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed to protect/extract depending on the card and opponent’s line.

I started the hand with a premium holding (AQo) and raised from the Big Blind after Minni limped. The flop of 4c-Th-6h misses my range entirely. Minni, who usually checks to the raiser, has led out with a 'donk bet' of 600 into a pot of 800 (75% pot). This unusual aggression from her in this spot typically indicates a strong Ten, a made hand like a middle pair, or a significant draw (like a flush draw or straight draw) that she wants to protect or build a pot with. While I have position and two overcards, I have no hearts in my hand to provide backdoor flush equity, and the board is quite coordinated. Calling 600 chips with just Ace-high against a large lead is a high-variance play that often leads to difficult decisions on the turn. Given the current stage of the game (Hand 47 of 150), I prefer to fold and wait for a better spot rather than chasing 6 outs against a range that likely has me beaten currently.

2-J offsuit is a weak, unprofitable holding from UTG, the earliest position at the table. I'm facing a 100 chip call to a 150 chip pot. However, position is critical in poker, and playing weak hands out of position from UTG is a fundamental leak in game theory optimal play. 

With 4 opponents still to act behind me, there's significant risk of facing a raise. Even if called, 2-J plays poorly post-flop with limited equity against a wide range of hands. The effective stack depth (9180) gives opponents room to apply pressure, and my UTG opening range should be tightly compressed to premium holdings.

Standard UTG opening ranges include high pairs, AK, AQ, KQ, and strong broadway hands - not 2-J. This hand should be folded without hesitation to preserve fold equity and maintain a tight UTG image.

I have Tc2d on the button — essentially junk. There are two players left to act (SB and BB) who have shown willingness to defend and 3‑bet; raising to steal with this hand risks facing aggression out of position or playing multiway postflop with almost no equity or playability. Calling 100 is cheap but likely creates a multiway pot where T2o performs very poorly. With deep stacks I should conserve chips for better spots and avoid marginal pots I can’t play well postflop. Fold and wait for a stronger opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 7d. Since the action folded to me, it's a battle against Elon in the Big Blind. While 87o is a marginal hand, it has some connectivity and is a reasonable candidate for a steal attempt or to build the pot in a head-up situation. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take the pot pre-flop.

T5o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity against a SB raise range (which is wide but still favors calling/raising hands), and terrible implied odds OOP. Pot odds are about 3:1 (200 to call into 400 pot), but folding trash preserves stack for better spots. No history suggests Flash is super loose here; standard fold from BB.

I'm UTG in a 4-handed game holding T♠2♣ — a weak offsuit, unconnected hand with very little post‑flop playability. Calling 100 into a 150 pot would commit chips out of position against three opponents (Flash BTN 8950, Elon SB 7500, Claude BB 9080) and likely put me in multiway pots where my equity and fold equity are poor. Raising for a steal with this hand is also inappropriate — it plays badly if called and has almost no redraw potential (only a deuce backdoor). With deep stacks and aggressive players behind, the correct long‑term play is to preserve my stack and fold preflop.

I am on the button with Q5o. While the button is the best position, Q5o is a very weak hand. Minni has already folded UTG. The blinds are Elon and Claude. Elon has shown some aggression in previous hands, and Claude is a solid player who defended his big blind well. With a stack of 89.5 big blinds, there is no need to play such a marginal hand from this position, especially since a raise would likely be met with resistance from the blinds or forced to play post-flop with a weak range. I will fold.

Preflop in SB with 94o offsuit after folds from UTG and Button. This is a steal spot vs BB, but 94o has poor postflop playability, low equity against a calling range, and no suited/connected value for multiway or implied odds. Stacks are deep enough that folding weak hands preserves chips for premium spots. Standard fold to avoid tough postflop situations OOP.

I am in the UTG position with 9c 4h, which is a very weak hand in a four-handed game. My stack is healthy at 8950, but playing trash hands from early position is a losing strategy. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Folded to me on the button with A7o, an ace-high offsuit hand that plays well postflop due to ace dominance and decent equity against typical calling ranges. Position is excellent, stacks are deep, and stealing blinds or isolating is standard here. Raising to 3x the big blind (300) builds the pot with initiative while keeping sizing reasonable. History shows aggressive opponents but no action yet this hand.

Calling with Td9s from SB. This is a solid speculative hand with broadway cards that makes straights well. Pot odds are approximately 2.8:1, which is favorable. Elon's button raise from the aggressive player is likely a wide range. Position post-flop is decent (only BB behind), and Td9s plays well in multi-way pots. Cost is only 250 from a 9180 stack, which is reasonable. If Minni 3-bets, I can reassess, but standard play is to call and see the flop.

I’m in the big blind with J♣8♦ facing a button open to 300 and a small‑blind cold‑call — a 3‑way pot, out of position. To call I’d need 200 more into a ~700 pot, so my break‑even equity is ~22% (200/(700+200) ≈ 22.2%). J8o is a marginal, easily dominated hand versus a BTN open and an SB caller and has poor post‑flop playability OOP in a multiway pot. A light 3‑bet to 500 would bloat the pot OOP against two competent stacks and isn’t a good bluff spot here. I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack for better, more playable situations.

Top pair with strong 9-kicker on a relatively dry three-card flop. Heads-up with Elon who is shorter-stacked and aggressive. I should bet to take control, get value from weaker hands, and protect my hand. Standard 2/3 pot C-bet of around 450 is appropriate sizing. This charges draws and weaker pairs while allowing premium hands to respond.

Preflop, raised with A7o from button, standard steal/isolation play. Claude calls from SB, then donks 450 into ~700 pot on QsTs3h flop. This board hits his calling range decently with Qx, Tx, but donk bet OOP suggests polarized range: weak value like mid pairs/draws or bluffs, less likely nuts like sets/AQ/AK which might check-raise. My Ah7c is ace-high with backdoor flush draw potential, solid blocker to strong Ax hands. Pot odds 450 to win 1600 (~22% equity needed), and ace-high has ~25-35% equity vs typical donk range (pairs + draws). Deep stacks, excellent position to realize equity and bluff later streets if needed. Calling keeps pot manageable, extracts value if he weak, sets up turns where I can take initiative. Raising pure air risks bloating pot OOP opponent's range when called; folding too tight given history and blockers.

Betting 900 on the turn with top pair (Td9s) against Elon's calling line. The 5d is a blank that doesn't significantly change the board dynamics. Elon is shorter-stacked (6750) and called my flop aggression, suggesting a moderate holding. Continuing to build the pot while ahead is the correct strategy here. This bet sizing applies reasonable pressure while representing consistent strength from the flop through the turn.

Facing a second barrel from Claude on the turn after calling his flop donk. Board is 3h Qs Ts 5d, uncoordinated with straight and flush draws possible. My Ah7c is ace-high with a backdoor heart draw that didn't hit, but strong ace blocker reduces his Ax combos. Preflop: BTN raise iso called by SB. Flop donk-call justified by position, equity vs polarized range (Qs/Ts value + spade draws/gutshots). Turn 5d is a brick for most draws, but he continues betting 900 (~60% pot) into 1600, representing continued polarization: thin value (top pair, stubborn sets) or missed draws turning into bluffs. Pot odds 900 to win 2500 (26.5% equity needed); ace-high has ~30-40% vs bluff-heavy continuation range given his history of aggression (e.g., Hand 1 river value, Hand 16 turn bet fold equity). Deep stacks (eff. ~5800 post-call), position allows controlling pot and bluffing/hero-calling river. Raising air risks vs calling station or traps; folding too tight given blockers/odds/history. Call to realize equity, pot control.

River check with pair of tens and 9-kicker on a 3h-Qs-Ts-5d-Jh board. Elon is short-stacked (5850) and has called two rounds of aggression (flop and turn), suggesting a moderate holding. The Jack on the river is a potentially dangerous card that could have improved many hands to two pair (AJ, KJ, AT, etc.) or made other pairs stronger. By checking, I accomplish several things: (1) Extract value by allowing Elon to check back with weaker hands (AK, KQ without a pair, drawing hands), (2) Avoid getting blown off the best hand if Elon shoves over a bet, (3) Keep the pot manageable given his short stack and my chip lead (7580 vs 5850). If Elon bets, I have information to make a fold/call decision based on his bet size. If he checks, I showdown with a strong hand that likely wins against his range.

River board 3h Qs Ts 5d Jh completes some heart draws (3h Jh), possible Jx hands, and straights like K9 (9-T-J-Q-K). My Ah7c is ace-high no pair, strong blocker to AQ/AJ and nuts, solid bluff-catcher after calling flop/turn donks. Claude's line (SB call pre, flop donk 450/~850 pot, turn bet 900/~1750 pot, river check) is strong: likely thin value (Qx/Tx top pair, two-pair QT/QJ/TJ) or stubborn draw that bricked/marginalized. Checking river shows weakness, induces calls from J-high/worse or showdowns missed draws. Preflop BTN iso + calls rep polarized range (Ax/draws/sets); betting risks (half-pot ~1700) fold equity vs value or calls from better (QJ/QT/TJ/Jx straight draws), poor vs calling station tendencies in history (Hand 2 river call, Hand 14 multi-street). Deep effective stacks (~5800), pot control maximizes by checking behind to win vs bluffs/J-high/weak Qx without showdown risk. History shows Claude mixes aggression/folds (Hand 11 turn fold, Hand 16 turn bet fold equity); check realizes equity optimally.

UTG position pre-flop with 6s2s, a very weak suited hand with poor connectivity and low cards. Early position requires tight range. No pot odds or steals worth considering here with blinds and three opponents behind. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in this long-term game.

Pocket sevens from the button is a strong hand worth playing aggressively. With Elon having folded UTG, we're in a 3-player situation against Minni (SB) and Flash (BB). A standard 3x BB raise to 300 is the correct sizing here. This accomplishes multiple objectives: (1) It's profitable to take down the blinds immediately if they fold, (2) It builds the pot with a good hand, (3) It keeps my range balanced as I've been relatively tight at the button, and (4) Pocket pairs play better when we get heads-up or can flop a set with decent pot odds. Both opponents have solid stacks, so they may call, but I'm in position and this is a standard value raise with a strong holding from the button.

Facing a button open to 300 from the small blind with A♣5♠: this hand has decent showdown value and an ace-blocker, and calling 250 more (300 total in pot) keeps the pot controllable while preserving stack depth. A small iso-raise is marginal (and invites 3-bets or multiway pots OOP), and folding is too tight given the pot odds and the chance to play heads-up if the big blind folds. I’ll call and proceed cautiously on the flop—looking to continue only with top pair/strong draws or to release to heavy aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh 4c, a relatively weak hand. Claude raised from the Button to 300, and Minni called from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 700 and it costs me 200 more to call. While the pot odds (3.5 to 1) are somewhat tempting, Qh 4c is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position in a three-way pot. Given the strength shown by both opponents and my position, folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable play to preserve my stack.

Check. I'm out of position with A♣5♠ on 9♠-6♥-3♥ and only have Ace-high plus a very remote backdoor wheel; no real draws and limited fold equity as the preflop caller. Claude opened from the button so his c-bet range will be wide — pot control is preferable. By checking I keep the pot manageable, can call small-to-medium continuation bets to realize my showdown value (and re-evaluate on the turn), and will take down the pot if he checks back. I will call modest bets (~≤1/2 pot ≈350) but be prepared to fold to large aggression (>~2/3 pot ≈470) unless I improve on the turn.

I have pocket sevens, which is an underpair to the 9 on the board. The flop of 9s-6h-3h is relatively dry, but I'm holding the second-best pocket pair possible in this situation. Minni checked after calling my preflop raise, which indicates weakness or a marginal holding. In position with an underpair, I should take a cautious approach and check here to control the pot size. This keeps my line balanced - I can still maintain aggression on safer turn cards if one comes, or I can fold cheaply if the board becomes dangerous. Betting here risks building a pot when I'm vulnerable, while checking allows me to see the turn card and reassess. The two-heart board also means Minni could have various flush draws, so keeping the pot smaller right now makes sense. I'll see what the turn brings while staying in position.

Turn: 9♠ 6♥ 3♥ 3♦. I have A♣5♠ (only ace-high), pot 700, and there's currently no bet. Claude opened preflop to 300 and checked the flop — that looks like a wide/weak or cautious range. I have almost no equity (only backdoor pair outs to A or 5), no meaningful draws, and I’m out of position. Betting as a pure bluff here is high-risk: many hands in his checking range will call or raise, and I have little fold equity or redraw potential. Better to control the pot and keep my stack depth for later streets. Plan: check and see his action. If he fires a small bet (≈≤1/2 pot ≈350) I’ll call to realize showdown value; if he puts significant pressure (>~2/3 pot ≈470) I’ll fold unless I improve on the river. If he checks back we go to a cheap river where I can reassess.

On the turn with pocket sevens on a 9s-6h-3h-3d board, I'm in a good spot to apply pressure. Minni has shown significant weakness by checking both the flop and turn, suggesting they either have a weak pair, a missed draw, or marginal hand. While the 3d on the turn creates some risk (pairing the board), my 7s are likely ahead of most hands in Minni's calling range from the SB. A bet of 400 (roughly 2/3 pot) accomplishes several goals: (1) applies pressure to weaker holdings that Minni may fold, (2) builds the pot when I'm likely ahead, (3) is proportional to my stack and pot size, and (4) maintains aggression with a strong hand in position. This sizing is standard and puts Minni in an uncomfortable spot if they truly have a weak hand, while also extracting some value if they do call with hands like 5s, 4s, or undercards that are behind. The river can still bring action, but I'm taking my opportunity to apply pressure now rather than letting Minni catch up for free.

I have A♣5♠ on 9♠-6♥-3♥-3♦. Pot is 1100 and Claude bets 400, so calling costs 400 into a 1500 total (1100+400). I only have 6 outs (three Aces + three 5s) to pair by the river ≈ 6/46 ≈ 13% equity. The break-even equity to call is 400/(1100+400) ≈ 26.7%, so a call is -EV. A bluff-raise would require very high fold equity (and would commit more chips OOP on later streets); Claude’s line (button open, checked flop, now betting a paired turn) is consistent with many hands that beat Ace-high (pairs, trips, or thin value), so I don’t expect sufficient fold frequency. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

T8o (Ten-Eight offsuit) is a weak, unconnected hand with limited playability. From UTG (first to act), I should be playing a tight, strong range. Even though the cost to call is only 100, this hand lacks the equity and potential to justify entering the pot from early position. With a decent stack and plenty of hands remaining (52 of 150), I can afford to be selective and wait for higher-quality holdings. This is a clear fold.

Button with Ah9c — a playable, positionally strong hand. Pot is 150 and I can either limp for 100 or open; I want to take the initiative and apply pressure to the blinds while keeping the sizing standard for this table. A 3x-ish open to 300 gives good fold equity against the SB and BB, keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks, and discourages too many loose callers. If I face a big 3-bet from Elon I can fold or reassess, but versus routine calls I can play postflop in position with a healthy range advantage. Raise to 300.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Deuce of clubs. This is a strong suited-Ace that plays well in multi-way pots or against a button open. Minni opened for 300 from the button, which is a standard opening size. While I could 3-bet here to take advantage of the fact that Minni has folded to my 3-bets previously (Hands #21 and #23), calling is a lower-variance play that allows me to see a flop with a hand that has nut-flush potential. Elon in the Big Blind is relatively short-stacked, so I'll call and see how the board develops.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 (3bb) and SB call. Pot 700, need 200 to call (getting ~3.5:1). Hand 93o is extremely weak: offsuit, unconnected, low cards, dominated by raiser's range. Minni aggressive on button, Flash calling flat. Multiway OOP with trash is -EV. No raise incentive (no fold equity with this). Fold preserves stack for better spots.

On a paired board like 8s-Kc-Kh, the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) often has a range advantage, but I have the nut kicker for an Ace-high hand and a backdoor nut flush draw. Being out of position, I will check to the button to see how they react to this board. Leading out into the pre-flop raiser here doesn't make much sense unless I'm trying to represent the King, but it's better to play defensively and see if they continuation bet.

I opened to 300 from the button and got one caller in the small blind, so my c-bet is a normal option. The flop Kc Kh 8s is a paired, high-card board that actually reduces my hand’s raw equity (Ah9c has only Ace-high and no realistic draws) but as the preflop aggressor I still have a range advantage. A small continuation bet can fold out many of the SB’s missed hands (small pairs, weak A/x, broadways) while keeping the pot controllable if I get raised. I don’t want to overcommit with a marginal bluff against a spot where a call or check-raise often denotes a made King, so I’ll bet small — roughly 1/3 of the pot — to take the initiative and pick up the pot now most of the time. Plan: fold to a sizable raise; if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and only continue with a real improvement or a very safe card.

On a paired board of 8s-Kc-Kh, Minni is making a small continuation bet of 250 into 700. My Ace-high (Ac-2c) currently has significant showdown value against her air bluffs and I have the nut backdoor flush draw. The price offered (250 to call into a pot that will be 1200) is very good. Raising here is unnecessary as I want to keep her bluffs in and I'm not yet sure if she has the King. I will call to see the turn.

On the turn, the Ten of Spades completes some straight draws (like QJ) and improves hands like JT or T9 that Minni might have opened with. I still only have Ace-high. While I have the nut kicker, betting here doesn't achieve much as I'm unlikely to fold out a King or a Ten, and I want to keep her bluffs in the pot. Checking is the most prudent play to see how she reacts to the turn card and to potentially reach showdown cheaply.

I opened pre‑flop and c‑bet a small 250 on Kc Kh 8s and got one call from the SB. The turn is the 10s and Flash checks — I have Ah9c: ace‑high only, no pair and no real draw. My prior plan was to proceed cautiously on the turn unless I improved or saw a very safe card; the 10 doesn’t improve me and actually helps some of his calling hands (10x, some pairs) while leaving Kx and medium pairs squarely ahead. A bluff here would need a fairly low fold frequency (~25% for a ~400 bet into 1,200) and Flash has shown willingness to call down. I’ll control the pot and go to showdown by checking behind; if he fires the river I’ll evaluate based on sizing (call small probing bets because of my Ace blocker, fold to large pressure).

On the river 3h, the board is 8s-Kc-Kh-Ts-3h. I have Ace-high with Ac-2c. After Minni checked back on the turn, it is likely she has some marginal showdown value like a small pocket pair (44-77) or an 8, or she has completely given up with air. My Ace-high beats her pure bluffs and missed straight draws like QJ or J9, but loses to any made pair. There is very little value in betting here as I won't be called by worse hands, and I'm unlikely to fold out a pair of 8s or better given the line taken. Checking is the most standard play to reach showdown and see if my Ace-high is the best hand.

I check to go to showdown. I opened preflop and c‑bet the flop; Flash called and then both of us checked the turn. With Ah9c I never improved — I have only ace‑high and no realistic redraw — and the board (K K 8 T 3) hits a lot of hands in his calling range (any K for trips, 10x, 8x, or small pairs). A river bluff would need a high fold frequency to be +EV against a player who willingly called the flop, and given the pot size (1,200) I don't expect enough fold equity to risk chips. Checking preserves my stack and lets me show down the best hand I have without throwing more away; if Flash had bet a small probe I’d consider a call only for very cheap bluffs, but since he checked I’ll check behind.

UTG with J♣5♥ — a weak, unsuited rag with poor playability out of position. Calling 100 to play OOP against the Button, SB and BB (who have shown willingness to defend and 3-bet) is a low-EV play. Raising to 200 as an UTG steal is too loose here and will often get 3-bet or cold-called by stronger ranges; with deep stacks that becomes a difficult multistreet problem. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot in position. Fold.

I am on the button in a 4-handed game. Ace-Trey offsuit is a standard opening hand from this position to apply pressure to the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. I will raise to 300, which has been the consistent opening size in this game.

9c7c is a marginal suited connector with some playability due to flush and straight potential, but I'm out of position in the small blind facing a button raise from Flash, who may have a wide stealing range. Post-flop OOP is tough with this hand, especially multiway if BB calls. Pot odds are about 1.8:1, but implied odds are uncertain given my 5700 stack and opponents' deep stacks. Better to fold and wait for stronger hands or better positions to maximize EV long-term over 150 hands.

4-10 offsuit is a weak hand that doesn't warrant defending against a button raise in the big blind. Flash has been aggressive, but I don't have the equity to call here. With only 200 to call into a 450 pot, I'd need significant equity, but 4-10o is dominated and will struggle postflop out of position. This is a clear fold—I should preserve my stack for better spots where I have stronger holdings.

In a 4-handed game, UTG is effectively the Cutoff. A8o is a standard opening hand in this position. I will raise to my usual 3bb to take control of the pot and potentially take the blinds or see a flop with initiative.

Js5h is a trash hand with poor playability and low equity against an UTG raise from Flash, whose range is strong (pairs, broadways, suited aces/connectors). On the button, I have position, but calling 300 into 450 pot (pot odds ~1.5:1) is poor as I'll be dominated often and struggle postflop. 3-betting has low fold equity vs UTG and I'm not deep enough to apply pressure effectively (57bb stack). Folding preserves stack for better spots in this multiway pot potential.

K6o is too marginal to call an UTG raise from the small blind. While pot odds are 1.8:1, I'm out of position post-flop and K6 has poor equity against Flash's likely range. Minni also has pending action, creating further uncertainty. Better to preserve chips and wait for premium hands.

Facing an UTG open to 300 (pot 450, 200 to call). I’m in the big blind out of position with Jd2s — a weak, easily dominated hand with very poor post‑flop playability. To call I need ~30.8% equity (200/(450+200)), and J2o has well under that vs a standard UTG raising range. 3‑betting as a bluff here would be overly risky with almost no fold equity and will often put me in very tough OOP decisions. With a healthy stack I’d rather conserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

82 suited is a speculative hand with poor playability out of position from UTG in a 4-handed game. It performs poorly multiway and doesn't warrant a raise or limp preflop. Standard fold to preserve stack for better opportunities, especially with 57BB stack and deep opponents.

I'm on the button with Ad4d (ace-high with a four kicker). While this is a weak hand, I'm getting favorable pot odds (150 to call into 350 pot = 3:1) and excellent positional advantage against the blinds. Elon has already folded, leaving only Minni (small blind, 14420) and Flash (big blind, 8500) in contention. My stack is healthy at 11230. The Ad4d has potential as a flush draw, and playing in position post-flop with two relatively shorter stacks allows me to leverage my positional advantage effectively. Calling 100 to take a flop is profitable given my position and implied odds. This is consistent with my aggressive but selective approach to position-based play.

2c8h offsuit is one of the worst hands to play, and I'd be out of position against the button and big blind for the whole hand. Although calling only costs 50 into a 250 pot (cheap), this hand is frequently dominated and has poor post‑flop playability, especially multi‑way. A squeeze/raise would be a fancy bluff here but unnecessary and too risky with players behind who can call or 4‑bet. I’ll fold and preserve stack for a better spot in position with a real hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 4h 3h. Claude, the Button, has limped in. Minni in the Small Blind has folded. I have the option to check or raise. 43s is a decent speculative hand with straight and flush potential, but playing it out of position against Claude (who has a deep stack) isn't ideal for a large raise. Since I'm already in the pot and have no extra cost to see the flop, I will check to see a flop for free and realize my equity.

I have 4-high on a board of 9-K-Q, which offers no immediate improvement to my hand. The board is very high-card heavy and likely connects with many hands Claude might limp in with from the button (Kx, Qx, J10, etc.). I have a backdoor heart flush draw but virtually no current equity. I will check and see what Claude does; if he bets, I will fold.

Semi-bluff bet with a 9-outer flush draw. Flash's flop check indicates weakness. My position and draw equity make this an aggressive but mathematically sound play. A bet of 165 (66% pot) applies pressure to Flash's shorter stack while maintaining value with my drawing hand. If Flash folds, I win immediately; if they call, I have 36% equity to improve on the turn or river.

I have 4-high on a board of 9-K-Q with only a backdoor heart flush draw. This board heavily favors a standard button limping range (which often includes Broadway cards or high-card combinations). Claude's bet of 165 into a 250 pot is a standard continuation/probation bet in a limped pot. Since I have no real equity or reasonable path to winning the hand without a massive bluff, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Folding 5-3 offsuit in UTG. This is universally the worst hand in poker with no equity potential, no position, and I can't win it preflop. With three opponents remaining to act and early position weakness, there's no value in speculating with this trash hand. Preserve chips for premium hands.

I open-raise to 300 from the button with 7♣4♣. In 4-handed cash-game play the button should be opening a wide range — 7-4 suited is marginal but playable in position because of fold equity and postflop playability. Pot is 150 and calling 100 (limping) surrenders too much fold equity and invites multiway action with a weak hand. A raise to 300 (a standard 3x-sized open here) maximizes fold equity vs the small blind (Flash has folded to raises often) and still keeps the sizing reasonable vs the big blind (Elon) who defends but will 3-bet only sometimes. Plan: fold to a large, committed 3-bet; if we get called we'll use position — c-bet on dry boards, play cautiously on coordinated/wet boards, and look to realize equity and exploit positional advantage. This sizing balances steal equity and postflop flexibility while protecting our stack.

I am in the Small Blind with K3o. Minni opened to 3bb (300) from the Button. While K3o has some high card value, it is a very weak hand to play out of position against a button raise, especially with a short-stacked player in the Big Blind (Elon) who might jam over the top. Calling 250 more to play a weak hand out of position is generally a losing play. Folding is the most prudent action here to preserve chips for better spots.

76o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability out of position against a button raise from Minni, who raises frequently. Pot odds are about 2.25:1 to call 200 more into 650, but the hand has low equity multiway potential or postflop, vulnerable to domination. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this deepstack game.

UTG with 8h8s — a solid, playable medium pocket pair. With 145bb stacks and 3 players to act behind, I want to build value and shrink the field (pocket 8s lose equity multiway). Standard open would be ~2.5–3x BB, but I’ll size a bit bigger to discourage limp-calls and isolate the button or big blind. Raising to 400 (4x) gives good fold equity, keeps maneuverability postflop, and extracts value from worse hands. Plan: proceed postflop with continuation bets on favorable textures; re-evaluate if facing a sizable 3-bet (adjust to stack/actor — fold to an oversized 4-bet, call or 3-bet-turn vs standard 3-bets depending on opponent).

I am on the button with K9s, a strong suited hand that plays well post-flop. Minni has opened from UTG (the first position in this 4-handed game) for 4x the big blind. While she has been active, a 4x raise suggests a solid range. However, K9s is too strong to fold on the button and functions well as a calling hand to keep the pot manageable while retaining positional advantage for the rest of the hand. I will call and see a flop.

Qs5d is a trash hand with no playability, offsuit and gapped. Facing an UTG raise to 4x from Minni, who often raises wide but still a strong range, and a button flat from Flash. I'm OOP in SB with poor postflop prospects and shallowish stack relative to pot odds. Immediate fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I'm in the big blind with J2o, one of the weakest possible starting hands. Minni raised from early position (UTG) and Flash called, suggesting both players have reasonable holdings. I'm out of position and will face aggression postflop with a hand that struggles to make strong holdings. J2o has very limited playability, especially when I'm already committed and out of position against two opponents. The 3.2:1 pot odds don't justify calling with this marginal hand when better folding opportunities exist. Folding is the mathematically sound decision here.

I opened to 400 preflop with 8♣8♠ (pocket eights) and Flashed called from the button. Flop 6♣ Q♥ 9♠ is fairly dry but contains two overcards to my pair, so my medium pocket pair is vulnerable if I give free cards. Out of position heads‑up I prefer to c‑bet to both extract value from worse pairs/draws and deny equity to backdoor/straight draws and overcards. I size small (~1/3 pot) to keep the pot manageable versus a wide calling range but still charge hands that missed. Plan: bet 300; if he folds I take it down, if he calls I reevaluate on the turn (check/control if an overcard hits, barrel small on safe cards); if he raises big I will generally fold to avoid bloating the pot with only middle pair, but I may call small raises to see a turn given deep stacks.

I have middle pair (Nines) with a King kicker. The board is Q-9-6, and Minni has led out for 300 into a pot of 950. This is a small bet (about 1/3 pot), and while she could have a Queen or a higher pocket pair, she could also be continuation betting with air or a draw. My hand is too strong to fold given the price and my position, but not strong enough to raise for value. I will call to keep the pot manageable and use my positional advantage on the turn.

I opened to 400 preflop with pocket 8s and c‑bet the flop 300; Flash called. Turn brings 4♠ to make the board 6♣ Q♥ 9♠ 4♠. This is a relatively “safe” low card — it doesn’t pair the overcards (Q/9) that dominate my hand, though it does add a second spade. I’m out of position with a medium pair and deep stacks, so I want to balance extracting value from floats/worse pairs and not bloating the pot when I’m often behind to Qx/9x. A small continuation bet (~1/3 pot) charges floating hands and backdoor draws while preserving fold equity and pot control. I will bet 500 into 1550; if he folds I take the pot, if he calls I re‑evaluate on the river based on the runout, and if he raises big I will generally fold (avoiding committing with only middle pair).

I currently have middle pair (Nines) with a King kicker. Minni has led out for two streets with relatively small sizings (roughly 1/3 pot on both the flop and the turn). The 4s on the turn is a blank unless she's specifically holding 57s. My hand is too strong to fold to such small bets, as I still beat all of her bluffs and semi-bluffs (like J10 or 87). However, I don't have enough value to raise, as most hands that call a raise will have me beat (like a Queen or an overpair). I will call to maintain pot control and see the river.

Check. My hand is a medium pair (pocket 8s) and the river pairing the board to Q♣ makes a big portion of Flash’s calling line (he called preflop, called a flop c‑bet and called my turn bet) significantly stronger — top pair (Qx) now becomes trips and 9x/66/44 also beat me. A river bet here will rarely fold out the hands that beat me and will mainly get called by them, so betting for value is thin and only bloats the pot. I want pot control and to avoid turning this into a large bluff‑catcher spot; checking gives me showdown versus bluffs/missed hands and lets me fold to a sizeable river shove from a value hand. Plan: check; if Flash bets a very small blocker size (≈≤25–30% pot, ~≤600) I may call as a block/call against a wide bluff frequency, but I will fold to medium/large value bets since his line heavily favors hands that beat my pair.

I have two pair, Queens and Nines, with a King kicker on a board of 6c-Qh-9s-4s-Qc. Minni, who led the betting on the flop and turn, has checked the river. While my hand is relatively strong, the board pairing the Queen is a significant concern. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by many worse hands, as a Nine with a lower kicker or a mid-pocket pair may fold to further pressure. Conversely, if Minni is holding a Queen (trips), she may be checking to induce a bluff or check-raise. Given that I have solid showdown value and the pot is already decent sized, checking back is the safest and most consistent play to realize my equity without risking a check-raise from a superior hand.

I am in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. My hand is Jack-Trey offsuit (J3o), which is extremely weak and well outside any reasonable opening range. Standard poker strategy dictates folding this hand to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Preflop on the button with T6o, a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability and dominated by any calling range. Facing the big blind after UTG fold, the pot odds to call 100 into 150 are not compelling (about 2.5:1), and this hand has low equity against typical BB calling or 3-bet ranges. Opponents like Minni are aggressive but BB defend wide; still, folding weak hands preflop preserves stack for better spots in a long 150-hand game. No position advantage postflop justifies entering with this.

Raising to 300 with KJ from the small blind when only the big blind opponent remains is a strong aggressive play. This 3x raise applies proper pressure, builds a pot where I'm ahead, and allows me to potentially win the hand immediately or play post-flop in a favorable position. The hand strength and heads-up situation make aggression optimal here.

I’m in the big blind with T♥5♣ facing a 300 open from the small blind (Claude). Pot is 400 and it’s 200 to call — that gives me ~33% pot odds. Heads-up vs an SB open his range should be fairly wide, and T5o has enough equity and showdown value to defend rather than folding. I won’t 3-bet to 500 because this hand has poor playability and I’d be out of position postflop; calling keeps the pot small and lets me see a flop to evaluate. Plan: call to 300 total, check the flop and proceed cautiously — fold to heavy aggression on dry misses, continue with equity on favorable flops.

I'm in the small blind with KdJc against Minni's big blind. I raised pre-flop to 300 and Minni called, heads-up. On a flop of 5h-6d-Ad, this is the ideal spot for a continuation bet. I have two broadway overcards and should maintain the aggression from my pre-flop raise. The ace on the flop doesn't necessarily hurt my position - Minni's call doesn't indicate significant ace strength, and I have outs to top pair if a K or J hits. A ~400 chip bet (roughly 65% pot) is standard sizing to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable. This semi-bluff has both value and fold equity in a heads-up pot where Minni's range is likely broadway cards, small pairs, or draws.

Call 400. I have T♥5♣ on 5♥‑6♦‑A♦ — a weak made hand (pair of fives, ten kicker). Pot is 1000 and it’s 400 to call (~28% pot‑odds).
